serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of ground nutmeg is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 2.5 g), used as a flavouring spice in cooking, baking, or beverages.

nutrient_source.title: Nutmeg, ground — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170172)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for ground nutmeg used as primary nutrient reference. HPB Singapore values cross-referenced and are consistent; HPB values retained for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol. Saturated fat sourced from USDA SR Legacy (25.94 g/100g). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrients beyond HPB scope sourced from USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Spices — Analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate spice category; nutmeg GI estimated at 14 consistent with published spice GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: Ground nutmeg does not have a direct entry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 14 is an analogous estimate consistent with low-GI dried spice category values. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (14 × 49.29 × 0.025) / 100 ≈ 0.17, rounded to 0.2. At a 2.5 g culinary serving, the glycemic load is nutritionally negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (16 mg/100g) with favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (350 mg K : 16 mg Na)
hypertension.guidance: Ground nutmeg is suitable for individuals with hypertension when used in typical culinary quantities (≤2 g per dish); its very low sodium content and moderate potassium level support blood pressure management, and no sodium-related concern arises at normal spice doses.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (estimated 14), high dietary fibre (20.8 g/100g), and bioactive compounds (myristicin, eugenol) with insulin-sensitising properties
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ground nutmeg is appropriate for individuals with type 2 diabetes when used in standard culinary amounts (≤2 g per dish); the negligible glycemic load per serving, high fibre content, and evidence of insulin-sensitising phytochemicals support glycaemic control, though high-dose supplemental use (>5 g/day) should be avoided due to myristicin toxicity risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat at 25.94 g/100g is not clinically relevant at culinary serving sizes (≤2 g per dish contributes <0.52 g saturated fat); anti-inflammatory phytochemicals may confer modest benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ground nutmeg poses no meaningful cardiovascular risk at typical culinary doses (0.5–2 g per dish), as the absolute saturated fat contribution is negligible; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid high-dose nutmeg supplementation and nutmeg-flavoured processed products that may contain added saturated fats or sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Eugenol and myristicin inhibit COX enzymes and reduce pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); high fibre and calcium provide additional RA-supportive benefits at culinary doses
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ground nutmeg may be used freely in culinary quantities by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its anti-inflammatory phytochemicals (eugenol, myristicin) and high fibre content are beneficial, while the high per-100g saturated fat is not representative of actual intake at typical spice doses of 0.5–1 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ground nutmeg is a warm, aromatic spice produced by drying and finely grinding the seeds of Myristica fragrans, an evergreen tree native to the Banda Islands of Indonesia. The resulting powder is a fine, light-to-medium brown colour with a distinctively warm, sweet-spicy, and slightly woody flavour profile. It is used globally as a culinary spice in both sweet and savoury applications, including baked goods, custards, béchamel sauce, mulled wine, curries, and rice dishes. Nutritionally, ground nutmeg is energy-dense at 525 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content (36.31 g/100g, predominantly saturated at ~25.94 g/100g), moderate carbohydrates (49.29 g/100g), and notable dietary fibre (20.8 g/100g). However, because it is consumed in very small culinary quantities (typically 0.5–2 g per dish), the per-100g figures are not representative of actual dietary intake. A 2.5 g teaspoon serving contributes approximately 13 kcal and less than 0.65 g saturated fat. Ground nutmeg contains bioactive phytochemicals including myristicin and eugenol, which have demonstrated anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and insulin-sensitising properties in preclinical studies. Its glycemic index is estimated at 14 (low), and the glycemic load per teaspoon serving is nutritionally negligible (~0.2). Clinically, ground nutmeg is considered safe and appropriate for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease when used in standard culinary amounts. High-dose use (>5 g/day) should be avoided due to myristicin-related neurotoxicity risk. It is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: Is ground nutmeg good for diabetes?
Can I use nutmeg if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in a teaspoon of nutmeg?
What is the glycemic index of nutmeg?
Is nutmeg safe for heart disease?
How much nutmeg is safe to eat per day?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients sourced from HPB Singapore (confidence 0.95) with cross-reference to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170172), which is an approved primary source. Saturated fat and detailed fatty acid profile sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database spice category, clearly documented. All values are internally consistent and plausible for ground nutmeg.
notes: Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100g — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central; ground nutmeg is a plant-derived spice and contains no dietary cholesterol. Vitamin B12 and vitamin D are 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived food with no animal-origin components. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy for ground nutmeg. Saturated fat is high at 25.94 g/100g (predominantly trimyristin); however, at a typical culinary serving of 2.5 g, the absolute saturated fat contribution is approximately 0.65 g, which is not clinically significant. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.22), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.35), and all micronutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170172 to supplement HPB data. GI value of 14 is an analogous estimate; no direct University of Sydney GI Database entry exists for ground nutmeg. High-dose nutmeg consumption (>5 g/day) carries myristicin toxicity risk; all health guidance applies to standard culinary quantities only. The 'wholegrain' dietary tag in the source input has been removed as it is not applicable to a ground spice.
